stocks.note: Numbers as of market close April 7th.
stocks.news:
The ceasefire heard ‘round the world — the U.S. and Iran struck a two-week deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, and markets barely flinched. The S&P gained 8 hundredths of a percent. The real action was underneath.

Broadcom jumped 6% after landing deals to build next-gen AI chips for both Google and Anthropic. The AI build-out isn’t slowing — Anthropic alone is expected to hit $30B in revenue this year, triple its 2025 number.

Bill Ackman is going after Universal Music Group with a $64.4B bid — yes, the company that holds Taylor Swift’s catalog. And the weight-loss drug war just got cheaper: Novo Nordisk launched high-dose Wegovy at $399/month, undercutting Eli Lilly’s Zepbound by 40%.

economy.commentary:
The ceasefire sent oil into freefall — WTI crude plunged 16% to $94.73 as the Strait of Hormuz reopened. It’s a two-week deal while negotiations continue, but markets are betting it sticks. Gold surged past $4,800 (+3.37%), with investors hedging against the chance that peace talks collapse.

The cost squeeze is real. USPS is adding an 8% surcharge on packages starting April 26. Amazon is tacking on 3.5%. Airlines hiked bag fees $10. When shipping costs more, everything downstream gets pricier.

Used cars hit $25,000 on average — the most expensive since 2023. New cars at $49K are pushing buyers into pre-owned, but there’s nowhere cheap to hide. Gas at $4.50/gallon is nudging more drivers toward EVs.

The IMF is waving the stagflation flag — prices climbing while growth stalls. The 10-year eased to 4.30%, bond markets watching whether the ceasefire holds.

crypto.commentary:
Big money is flowing back into Bitcoin. Spot ETFs pulled in $471 million on April 6 — the strongest single day in over a month. BlackRock’s IBIT led with $182M, Fidelity’s FBTC added $147M. It was the sixth-biggest inflow day of 2026.

Venture capital is making a notable shift — AI is eating crypto’s lunch. Startups like OpenAI and Anthropic are seeing record revenue, giving VCs something crypto couldn’t: predictable returns. When the smart money moves, the narrative follows.

real_estate.homebuilder: The ceasefire gives rates a breather — 30-year at 6.44%, ticking up just 1 basis point as spring buyers gear up despite oil uncertainty.
real_estate.commentary:
Total inventory across our 16-city tracker held steady at 2,495. Coming soon listings at 272 and new listings at 182 — sellers are staging even as total supply stays flat.

📍 Fremont: Holding at 297 with 28 new listings and 58 coming soon. Spring sellers are active.

📍 Union City: Steady at 81 — still one of the tightest markets on the board.

📍 Oakland: 728 total with 41 new listings. The Bay’s biggest market keeps churning through inventory.

📍 Milpitas: 115 units but just 1 new listing. Activity here is all pendings converting, not new sellers entering.

📍 Newark: Sitting at 99 — right on the edge of double digits.

📍 Hayward: 219 total with 17 new. Consistent supply for buyers looking east.

Nationally, over 300,000 homes sold in March — up 3.7% from last year and 25% from February. That’s the strongest March in five years, proving higher rates haven’t scared off serious buyers.

But here’s the bigger picture: home prices have nearly doubled in a decade, up 87%. Mortgage payments now eat over 30% of median household income, up from 21% in 2019. The silver lining? Some analysts believe income growth may finally outpace home prices this year — the first time since the 2008 financial crisis.
